pages.text: A study to determine the degree of partial blending of reclaimed asphalt pavement (RAP) binder for high RAP hot mix asphalt Prashant Shirodkar a, Yusuf Mehta a,*, Aaron Nolan a, Khyati Sonpal a, Alan Norton a, Chris Tomlinson a, Eric Dubois a, Patrick Sullivan b, Robert Sauber c a Department of Civil Engineering, Rowan University, 201, Mullica Hill, Glassboro, NJ 08028, USA b Department of Chemical Engineering, Rowan University, 201, Mullica Hill, Glassboro, NJ 08028, USA c New Jersey Department of Transportation (NJDOT), NJ, USA article info Article history: Received 5 March 2010 Received in revised form 3 May 2010 Accepted 19 June 2010 Available online 13 July 2010 Keywords: Reclaimed asphalt pavement Recycling Partial blending abstract The objective of this paper is to provide a methodology for determining the degree of partial blending in high RAP mixtures. When RAP is mixed with virgin aggregates and virgin binder, partial blending of RAP binder occurs in the hot mix asphalt. Agencies limit the amount of RAP because the degree of blending between the RAP and the virgin materials is not known. The methodology provides a systematic approach for determining the degree of partial blending in the RAP mixture. The ability to accurately determine the degree of partial blending will help in determining the virgin binder content to be added in the mixture. The degree of partial blending measured from this procedure for 25% RAP by weight of aggregates with PG 70-28 and 35% RAP by weight of aggregates with PG 58-28 are 70% and 96% respectively. /C2112010 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. 1. Introduction The demand to utilize higher percentages of reclaimed asphalt pavement (RAP) in the construction of hot mix asphalt (HMA) pavements continues to rise [1]. Several states, including New Jer- sey, conduct Superpave mixture designs for the high RAP mixtures. This procedure consists of burning the asphalt from the RAP and considering the RAP as one of the aggregates (without any RAP bin- der). After the design binder content is determined, the virgin bin- der content is established by giving full credit to the RAP binder, assuming 100% blending of RAP binder. However, researchers have consistently shown that when RAP is mixed with virgin binder and aggregates, partial blending occurs [2], that is, something between 100% blending and the ‘‘black rock” effect occurs. The assumption of 100% working binder may produce an under-asphalted mixture, resulting in poor pavement performance. Several state agencies limit the amount of RAP that can be placed in the different layers of pavement [3]. One of the primary reasons the RAP usage is limited is because the degree of blending between the RAP and the virgin materials has not been quantiﬁed. The question that needs to be answered is how much of the RAP binder blends with the virgin binder. Is there 100% blending or 0% blending or somewhere in between? The answer is probably somewhere in between the two extremes. Therefore, there is a need to determine a methodology that quantiﬁes the degree of par- tial blending occurring in high RAP mixtures. The ability to quantify the amount of blending will be critical in determining the actual virgin binder content and grade that needs to be added to the mixture after the design binder content is ob- tained. Determining the amount of RAP binder that is available for blending with virgin binder is the primary focus of this paper. 2. Objective The objectives of the st

extracted and recovered using the AASHTO T319 procedure [10]. The RTFO G/C3 / sin(d) of extracted binder was conducted at 76 /C176C and 70 /C176C. This temperature selection for the mix prepared with 25% RAP by weight of aggregates with PG 70-28 virgin binder was the same as the high PG-grade of the virgin binder. From the testing results it was found that binder testing temperature did not af- fect the determination of degree of partial blending. Hence, further all testing were carried out at the same temperature (76 /C176C and 70 /C176C). The G /C3 /sin(d)o f RTFO binder was selected for two reasons: (a) The amount of binder required for a RTFOT sample can be obtained with one single extraction and recovery using the AASHTO T319 procedure [10] and (b) the binder properties at high temperatures are generally more sensitive to blending than low temperature test results [5]. The concept behind this procedure is that if there is 100% blending, the proper- ties of the blended binder on the virgin and RAP aggregates would be similar. If there is 0% blending, the properties of the blended binder around the RAP aggre- gates after mixing will be a blend of the RAP binder and the proportion of the virgin binder that coats the RAP aggregates. The properties of the binder at zero blending condition around the virgin aggregates will be the properties of the virgin binder only. The following section will explain how the zero percent blending condition is simulated. 4.7. Determination of the properties of the binder around the RAP aggregate under zero blending condition In zero blending condition, RAP binder acts like a rock, in that it does not coat other virgin aggregates. While preparing mix, virgin binder coats the RAP. When coarse virgin aggregates are extracted and recovered, theoretically only virgin binder would be expected. When ﬁne RAP aggregates are extracted and recovered, mixed RAP and virgin binder in certain proportion would be obtained. This proportion of RAP binder and virgin binder can be calculated by determin- ing ﬁlm thickness of RAP binder and virgin binder. The ﬁlm thickness of RAP bin- der calculated by Bailey’s method was found to be 10 micron using the following equation: Weight of effective asphalt binder around the aggregate ¼ AC=ð100 /C0 ACÞð 2Þ Film thickness ¼ weight of effective asphalt binder around the aggregate =ð1000 /C3 specific gravity of asphalt /C3 total surface area ð3Þ Here, a. Weight of effective asphalt binder around the aggregate is calculated in kg/ kg of aggregates. b. AC is asphalt content determined by extraction recovery method AASTHO T319 [10] and expressed in percentage (%). c. Speciﬁc gravity of asphalt is assumed as 1.02. d. Total surface area is determined as per Bailey’s method [11], it is sum of product of surface area factor and gradation (percent passing) of extracted aggregates and expressed in m 2/kg (calculated in Table 3 ). Average ﬁlm thickness of virgin binder around the RAP was assumed as 8 mi- cron. This assumption was also cross checked using Bailey’s Method. Film thickness of virgin binder for 4.4% asphalt content for the given RAP aggregate gradation was found to be approximately 8 micron. Hence, the ratio of the RAP binder and the vir- gin binder coating the RAP is 56:44 (10:8). The RAP binder and the virgin binder were mixed according to the above ratio. This proportioned binder was subjected to short term conditioning in RTFO test and the Superpave PG properties (RTFO G /C3 /si

carried out during separation process. This blended binder is tested for RTFO DSR without subjecting to RTFO simulation. Denominator is difference between virgin binder and proportioned virgin and RAP binder (for zero blending). Proportioning of virgin binder and RAP binder recovered from RAP below #8 sieve is carried out as per the proportioned determined in Section 4.7 of this paper. This unaged binder is subjected to RTFO simulation and tested for RTFO DSR. In Eq. (4), extend of aging for binders in numerator and denominator would not be similar, as one is carried out while preparation of mix and other is carried out by RTFO simulation. But it is not going to affect the determination of degree of partial blending as ratio is taken. Table 4 shows a comparison of binder stiffness (RTFO G/C3 sin(d)) values and degree of partial blending for 25% and 35% RAP by weight of aggregates. 5.2. Discussion The degree of partial blending of RAP is dependent upon many factors like aggregate temperature during mixing, grade of binder, RAP binder property, percentage of RAP, and virgin binder prop- erty. From the above results, it can be seen that the degree of par- tial blending is more for softer binder. Also, degrees of partial blending determined from different binder testing temperatures are similar. Hence, the degree of partial blending is independent of binder testing temperature. 5.3. Methodology The methodology to determine the degree of partial blending is: Step 1: Determine the binder content of the RAP and the grada- tion of the extracted aggregates. Step 2: Determine the Superpave PG properties (from AASHTO M320) of the RAP binder and the virgin binder. Step 3: Create a Superpave gradation for a given percentage of RAP (i.e. 25% and 35%), such that all the ﬁne aggregates (minus #8–2.36 mm) are RAP and all coarse aggregates (greater than #4–4.75 mm) are virgin aggregates. The Superpave gradation created in the lab will be similar to the JMF gradation for a given percentage of RAP. Step 4: The design binder content from the JMF could be consid- ered for the study. If the design binder content is not known, determine the design binder content (DBC) based on the Super- pave mixture design. Step 5: Coating study – mix the RAP and the virgin aggregates. The mixing process was kept as close as possible to the practice followed by the plant in terms of the mixing time, the mixing process, and the temperatures of the virgin aggregates and the RAP. Calculate the increase in mass of virgin aggregates before and after mixing to determine the ‘‘the approximate” amount of RAP binder that coated the aggregates. This will help in determining the virgin binder content. Or assume initial bin- der transfer of around 50%. Step 6: Create the mixture at the virgin binder content (VBC) determined from the following equation: Virgin binder content ¼ design binder content from JMF /C0 estimated RAP working binder where the RAP working binder depends on the degree of partial blending is determined from step 5 above. Step 7: Separate the coated virgin and RAP aggregates after mix- ing by conducting sieve analysis at the #4 and #8 sieves Step 8: Extract and recover the binder separately from the coarse virgin aggregates (plus #4) and ﬁne RAP aggregates (minus #8). Step 9: Determine the Superpave PG properties (from AASHTO M320 like RTFO G/C3 /sin(d)) of the blended binder on the RAP and the virgin aggregates. Step 10: Determine the proportion of RAP and virgin bind

f. Degree of partial blending is higher for PG 58-28 as compare to PG 70-28. g. The recommended methodology was successfully adopted to determine the degree of blending of RAP binder. 7. Signiﬁcance of study The methodology proposed in this paper provides a systematic approach of determining the degree of partial blending in RAP. The ability to accurately determine the degree of partial blending will help in precisely determining the virgin binder content to be added in a mixture. It will also help in developing a blending chart to determine the properties of the ﬁnal binder grade, the required vir- gin binder grade and the percentage of RAP based on the degree of partial blending measured from this procedure. 8. Limitations The limitations of the procedure: a. The process requires at least three extractions and recover- ies of the binder; these include the RAP before the blending study, the virgin aggregates and the RAP after the blending study. b. To minimize the heating during separation, prepared sam- ples should be large enough; heating should be done on por- tions of sample at a time which can be separated without cooling the sample. The sieving of the coated aggregates is very time consuming. 9. Conclusions and recommendations The study successfully demonstrated a proof of concept of accu- rately determining the degree of partial blending of RAP binder. The degree of partial blending for 25% RAP by weight of aggregates of chosen gradation with PG 70-28 virgin binder was found to be on an average of 70%. The degree of partial blending for 35% RAP by weight of aggregates of chosen gradation with PG 58-28 virgin binder was found to be on an average 96%. However, these results are depended upon various factors like RAP binder property, grada- tion, mixing temperature, virgin binder property, hence should not be generalized. From the above results, methodology to determine degree of partial blending is veriﬁed and this should be used to determine degree of partial blending of desired combination of materials. Laboratory investigation of mixing HMA with RAP. In: Transportation Research Board: Journal of the Transportation Research Board, no. 1929. Washington (DC): Transportation Research Board of the National Academies; 2005. p. 37–45. [3] Tam K, Marks P, Cautillo C. Results of survey on RAP usage. Material Engineer and research ofﬁce, Ministry of Ontario; 2008. [4] McDaniel RS, Soleymani H, Anderson RM, Turner P, Peterson R. Recommended use of reclaimed asphalt pavement in the SuperPave™ mixture design method. NCHRP Final Report (9–12), TRB, National Research Council, Washington, DC; 2000. [5] Kandhal P, Foo KY. Designing recycled hot mixture asphalt mixtures using superpave technology. NCAT Report no. 96-5; 1997. p. 7–22. [6] Al-Qadi IL, Carpentor SH et al. Determination of usable residual asphalt binder in RAP. Research Report ICT-09-031, Illinois Center for Transportation; 2009. [7] Bennert T,

Kandhal and Foo [5] at NCAT conﬁrmed the use of the three-tier system and also developed a ‘‘sweep blending chart” to determine the percentage of RAP if a three-tier system was not used. The ‘‘sweep blending chart” required the determination of G/C3 /sin(d) and G/C3 sin(d) for different percentages of virgin binder for high and intermediate temperatures. The percentage of RAP obtained by the intermediate temperature sweep blending chart (average 37%) was higher than the typical average practice of around 15– 20%. To rectify the discrepancy between the calculated percentage of RAP and actual practice, Kandhal and Foo [5] recommended a ‘‘speciﬁc grade” blending chart which has reduced the effort of developing three sweep blending charts. Huang et al. [2] blended RAP with virgin aggregates without any new virgin asphalt binder being introduced. The mixture was mechanically blended (dry blended). The purpose was to ﬁnd out the extent at which the aged asphalt from RAP particles would blend with virgin aggregate. In addition to the coating study, Huang et al. [2], attempted to determine the viscosity of RAP mixed with virgin binder by per- forming staged extractions. After the RAP was mixed with virgin binder, the RAP was dipped in trichloroethylene in stages and the solution was recovered. It was clear that the asphalt viscosity in- creased as it went from outside layers to the inside layers. Based on the staged extraction described in the study, it was observed that about 60% of the inner portion of the binder (nearest to the RAP aggregate) had asphalt properties close to pure RAP aged bin- der, whereas the outside 40% of the binders were blended with vir- gin binder. Al-Qadi et al. [6] conducted performance related tests for four blending cases, black rock effect (BR), total blending (TB), partial blending (PB) and actual practice (AP). The results conﬁrmed the study carried out by McDaniel and Anderson [4] who had studied blending of 10% and 40% RAP content. They found that at low RAP contents, the BR, TB, and AP cases were statistically similar but at high RAP contents, the BR case was different (TB and AP stayed statistically similar). Al-Qadi et al. [6] also concluded that the higher complex modulus of AP samples was either due to the selective absorption of lighter fractions in the aggregate surface over time or from the change in gradation caused by partial blend- ing (whose extent is unknown). Gradation change is caused either by formation of mastic layer or release of ﬁne particles in the RAP binder. Al Qadi et al. [6] made three mixes consisting of 0%, 20% and 40% RAP. In all the three cases, the overall gradation was kept the same. The Superpave mixture design of the above three mixes indicated that the binder content was the same. The sur- face area of the aggregates was similar for all the three mixes due to similar gradations. Due to similar surface area and binder content of all three mixtures, Al Qadi et al. concluded that 100% RAP binder was mobilized in all the three cases. However, the authors of this study believe that the binder content being the same does not necessarily conclude that the 100% RAP blending is occurring. Other studies to evaluate interaction between virgin and RAP binder is carried out by Bennert et al. [7]. Bennert et al. [7] devel- oped the analytical procedure, using back calculation methodology along with analytical methods developed by Bonaquist [8] and Rowe [9] to determine ‘‘effective” asphalt p

The mixing time of 10 min was selected by trial and error. After 10 min, no addi- tional change in visual coating of virgin aggregates was observed. Table 1 shows the batch weight of different aggregate bins and results of the coating study. As shown in Fig. 2 the increase in weight of the virgin aggregates is due to the coating by the RAP binder, however, the reduction in weight of the RAP aggregates may be due to four things (a) loss of moisture content, (b) RAP binder lost to bucket and arm, (c) loss of ﬁne particles of RAP during mixing, and (d) transfer of RAP binder to virgin aggregates. Therefore, the loss of RAP weight will be greater than the increase in the weight of virgin aggregates. This coating study without the virgin binder only pro- vides an estimate of the partial blending because some of the RAP working binder will also coat the RAP aggregates. Additionally, this cannot be measured in this pro- cess and the impact of the presence of hot virgin binder on the degree of partial blending cannot be captured. 4.3. Approximate RAP binder transfer The percentage of RAP binder in the RAP was obtained from the extraction and recovery process [10]. The percentage of binder in the RAP is 5.63%. The mass of bin- der in the RAP was determined from weight of RAP aggregates. The RAP binder coat- ing the coarse aggregates was determined from increase in weight of coarse virgin aggregates. The approximate RAP binder transferred was calculated based on the following equation. Table 1 shows the RAP binder transfer for 25% and 35% RAP averaged 24% and 15%, respectively. These values of binder transfer seem much lower than what is expected. Hence, it was approximated to higher end. Binder transfer was considered as 30% and 20% for 25% and 35% RAP. Approximate RAP binder transfer ð%Þ ¼ 100 /C2ð weight of RAP binder coating the virgin aggregates after mixing =weight of binder around the RAP before mixing Þð 1Þ 4.4. Blending study The binder content from the JMF was used in the design. A full Superpave mix design was not deemed necessary because the gradation was modiﬁed with the sole intent to determine the degree of partial blending. The optimum binder content from the JMF supplied by the plant was 4.8%. The approximate RAP binder transfer from the above coating study was used to determine the amount of virgin binder content. 4.5. Virgin binder content to be added based on the approximate RAP working binder Table 2 shows the weights of different aggregate bins by weight of mix (con- verted from weight of aggregates to weight of mix). The weight of total mix was se- lected such that sufﬁcient binder can be extracted for determining the binder properties. The detailed procedure used for the blending study with virgin binder is similar to the one outlined above except that the weights changed (shown in Table 2 ) and the virgin binder was also heated to mixing temperature. A schematic representa- tion of the procedure is shown in Fig. 3 . 4.6. Binder properties After mixing, the virgin aggregates were separated from the RAP aggregates by slight heating and manually separation. To minimize the heating duration, three batches of 5000 g mix were prepared. Also, while separating the mix, only a small portion of mix is heated in the oven.
